name: Coordinating sliding glass door replacement with seismic retrofits

mainEntity.name: If I’m planning a seismic retrofit in a Daly City or Pacifica home, should sliding glass door replacement be done before or after shear wall and foundation work?
mainEntity.text: If I’m planning a seismic retrofit in a Daly City or Pacifica home, should sliding glass door replacement be done before or after shear wall and foundation work?

acceptedAnswer.text: Large glass openings are critical elements in a home’s seismic performance, so sliding glass door replacements and earthquake retrofits should be coordinated, not treated as separate projects. If you plan to add or enlarge a slider in a Pacifica or Daly City home, these changes affect shear wall lengths and load paths. Ideally, the seismic retrofit design is completed first, showing which walls need bracing, hold‑downs, and anchor bolts. Once you know where shear walls will go, your contractor or design professional can decide whether the sliding door opening should shift, shrink, or stay as is. Replacing or resizing the door can then be scheduled during the retrofit framing phase, when walls are already open and new headers or shear panels can be installed in one coordinated effort. This avoids redoing finishes twice and reduces the risk of compromising newly installed structural work. If you replace a sliding door first and retrofit later, you may discover that the new opening conflicts with required bracing, forcing additional changes or limiting retrofit options. Talk early with your retrofit designer and general contractor about your plans for doors and windows so they can integrate everything into a single, code‑compliant plan.
acceptedAnswer.description: When planning both seismic retrofit and sliding door replacement, structural upgrades should be designed first, and door work coordinated with shear wall layouts. In many cases, major sliding door changes happen alongside or just after retrofit framing so headers, hold‑downs, and wall bracing around openings are built once to current seismic standards.
